video_title: Scaling Laws: Governing Transformative AI, with Christoph Winter and Charlie Bullock
transcript: Kristoff Winter and Charlie Bulock. Welcome to Scaling Laws. >> Happy to be here, Alan. >> Hello. >> So, we're going to talk about this terrific new paper that you have all recently released called radical optionality, which is your kind of really high level almost kind of meta perspective on how we should think about AI regulation and AI policy. So, let's just start by getting some definitions out on the table. What is radical optionality? Or maybe to break it down even further, what is optionality in this context and what makes your proposal radical? Let's start with Kristoff. >> Yes. So, as its core, uh, radical optionality is about preserving democratic government's ability to make good decisions about how to govern what we call transformative AI. Um, um, circumstances as these systems evolve over time. Um and it starts with a few assumptions. Um so first we think there is a real possibility that some version of transformative AI or whatever your preferred terminology is AGI, super intelligence, powerful AI, you name it. Um um um will be developed within the next 10 years. Um and second, we think there's profound uncertainty with regards to what capabilities uh um um will transformative AI systems um have um what what what risks and benefits they might they might pose um and and maybe uh even most importantly um how we might uh be able to govern these systems for the better. Um three uh we think that some uh regulation some form of governance of these powerful air systems will be necessary at some point. We have quite a bit of uncertainty as to how this governance regime should look like and how it should evolve over time. But we are pretty confident that we would want some regulation of this extremely powerful dual use technologies. And four, we believe that building the institutional capacity required to effectively govern transformative AI will take quite some time, will take a few years. Um, and given how quickly things are unfolding right now, uh, we think that governments would do well in starting building this institutional capacity right now. So let me let me pause on those four assumptions because it strikes me that the one that is really driving the bus as it were is the first one and that is about the inevitability or at least high likelihood of near-term transformative AI. It feels like once you accept that a lot of the rest of it follows and and I'm curious sort of first if you agree with that that sort of the the kind of >> as Claude would say loadbearing uh assumption here is um uh transformative AI and and two I'm curious you know obviously we all you know work in the same circles of people who are generally quite open to this possibility are pretty AGI pill one way or the other um but I'm curious as you all have been discussing this with people maybe outside the law AI lawfare bubble where this is kind of a um uh table stakes as it were maybe talking to policy makers or or people who are maybe more skeptical of AI um are you getting a lot of push back on this or or in 2026 has everyone sort of accepted that even if this is not inevitable it's sufficiently plausible now that you have to take stuff like this seriously it's not just a distraction from you know all the other issues you know water use and algorithm discrimination or whatever whatever else people really want to talk about. >> Yeah. I mean I I think that like >> the I I would say that for the first assumption is not uh we it doesn't it doesn't require assuming that it's like almost a certainty or even that it's very likely, right? It just means a non-negligible probability. I think the the the paper depends on the assumption that there's like at least a non-negligible probability, right? I don't know if that's 1% or 5% but there's some minimum amount of like okay this isn't totally implausible and I think the rest of it falls from there. even if you know I think uh I'm not sure how likely I think truly transformative AI is in the next 10 years but not 100% certainly um I I think a lot of people have updated you know towards a higher percentage in in recent months with mythos and things like that but um yeah I think uh the reaction I've gotten as I try to socialize this idea um mostly has been uh most people have accepted the first assumption because if you don't like um you're not even really in the same conversations or something, right? Like I I don't know why someone would be talking to me. Generally, they're not. I Kristoff might have a different experience if they don't accept at least the the bare possibility, right? Because >> I also don't know why anyone would be talking to you, but you know, here we are. [laughter] So I I don't think uh assumption one requires that there's like even a high likelihood or even say something like a 30% uh likelihood of transformative AI being developed in in the next 10 years or so. Um I think governments would do well implementing some version of radical optionality even if there was just a 5% probability of some version of transformative AI being developed sooner rather than later. I I I do think the probability is higher, but I think even if the probability was this low, um I I I I think the um the conclusions still follow from that. Um maybe to go back to your question on like how much do people buy into this? Uh so far, if you I I think there might be a difference between what people actually believe or they revealed preference and their stated preferencees. And I'm not quite sure yet what to make of this. Their stated preferences are are are that like a lot of people um we talk to buy into some version of that. They might not express it in the way that we express it. Um but but but they buy into some version of that. If you look um aside from like the more general population and various surveys there, if you just look into academic papers like the average paper starts off with something like oh AI will be a big deal yada yada yada. Um and and but but but but also when you look at what what uh academics are actually focusing on, what governments are actually doing, um the revealed preferences based on their action rather than what they state in the introductory sentences of a paper to make the case for why they write about this thing, they look still quite different. Um, and I think there was this there was this term a few years ago, um, I think it originated with IASK, um, that initially I found on the on the borderline cringy end of things of of feeling the AGI and I think by now um, I I like more closely or like better understand what was meant with that. Um and and it's a difference between sort of vaguely gesturing at or believing in this thing that will be quite powerful and will have a big impact on the world and actually having internalized it. Um and and and maybe sort of good rule of thumb or so for whether you fear the AGI or however else you you want to call this is like did you ever have a sleepless night about um um about how this technology might develop in the next 10 years. If you if you haven't, you probably don't feel it yet or so. Not everyone has to have a sleepless night over it. Uh but I I assume it correlates with like actually having internalized how big of a deal this actually is. >> I I love that idea. I think I think I think all of us who are in this space and and take this possibility seriously should reflect on what our Pauline road to Damascus moment is when we you know the scales fell from our eyes and we thought wow this is this is really uh really going to really might happen. Um okay so so radical optionality is meant to be responsive to the point that you all make that regulating AI is extremely hard even harder than all the sort of standard policy regulation challenges we have. So just jump in and Charlie let me start with you. What is so difficult what is so particularly difficult about AI that not only do we need optionality but it has to be radical. >> Sure. So um the sort of classic answer right the sort of classic problem in the regulation of emerging technologies right as sort of STS scholars talk about this all the time is the pacing problem right so our institutions and our laws develop quite slowly right over the course of time our constitutions develop even more slowly right over the course of centuries um and technology develops very quickly and AI in particular seems like it's uh developing you know sort of my qualitative assessment is that it's maybe even faster than fast technologies in terms of how quickly like um step changes and capabilities are occurring. Uh and combined with that uh is the fact that we don't understand the technology very well, right? Even the people who who create it, there's a sense in which there's a sense in which uh this technology is like uh poorly understood by them, right? It's a black box that's maybe a little bit outdated. Maybe like advances in interpretability mean that uh this isn't quite as true as it once was or something. But in a there there is a fundamental sense in which we do not understand the technology very well even though we're building it right. And so this creates a number of problems. Uh but essentially uh what you have is you have uh technology the capabilities of which are sort of expanding at an exponential rate and then you have uh a legal system and a uh governance regime that are currently non-existent because it's such a recent technology right and uh the stakes are so high because we're taking the transformative possibility seriously that like it might not be good enough to do what we've done with past emerging technologies and uh sort of wait around for 30 years until we understand what's going on and then make rules afterwards, right? Um, typically there's been this period, you know, if you whatever example you want to take, railroads, uh, airlines, etc. there's been this sort of period where like there's a very disorganized and ad hoc uh maybe state-based or or local uh effort at regulation and then eventually the the once there's some sort of understanding the big federal regime in the US or uh the more concerted approach to regulating technology uh comes into play you know decades later that might not be good enough this time around and so it it presents sort of unique challenges I would say >> so There are other approaches obviously to regulating AI, right? And you all place your position sort of in between what we might call and I'm going to simplify here, but let's call it the free market position on the right side, though. Again, I'm not sure how useful those labels are anymore, and the let's call it Eureaucrat position on on the other side. Uh Eureaucrat/CC California position. Sorry, Kristoff, I'm not trying to put you on blast here or anything. So, let let's go through each of them and you can te tell me uh why you think that that is sort of insufficient. So let's start with uh you know letter rip or or call it the sort of Andre and Horwitz uh or you know uh uh um uh position um around um you know permissionless innovation and letting the market figure this all out. Why isn't that the right answer to this technology? you know, given that it has these incredible benefits and that so far the market has done, you know, pretty impressive job pushing AI forward, why can't we just sort of uh abide by that approach? >> Okay. So, I think we've seen this uh evolution happen in real time over the past like 3 weeks or something in the United States, right? Um it was for a while the official White House position was the David Sachs position was uh you know deregulatory, right? It's essentially like we're cutting red tape and the efforts of the administration will be focused on removing regulatory obstacles imposed by the states rather than putting new regulatory obstacles in place. Um this is I think a defensible position with a technology generally speaking this is a defensible approach if you're a very you know free market filled person to a technology that does not have extremely serious national security implications. But there's almost no one short of like true like Ted Kazinski level anarchists, there's almost no one who believes that like national security is something that the market can handle, right? Like if uh no one suggests that like the proliferation of nuclear weapons, for example, is a topic that uh that uh we need zero regulation on, right? If everyone can have a nuke in their backyards, um then that seems bad and like it'll likely cause problems. And most people, even most committed libertarians, agree with this. Um so if we assume that there's a possibility that the military implications of transformative AI systems will be as profound and as you know dangerous as dual use as the implications of nuclear fishision which I think is like you know it to me seems obvious that that is that is it is at least possible that that will be true. um at that point you're no longer going to be able to have to let the market handle it, right? And I think we've seen this sort of realization come to people perhaps belatedly in the White House as you know the NSA says wait a minute these are extremely serious cyber security capabilities cyber offense capabilities that mythos has. We've got to get out in front of this. there are serious implications for national security and for the economy uh if we don't get ahead of this and that's why you've seen I think a pivot from a lot of people in the national security enterprise uh towards taking these things more seriously and suggestions of things that you know in my view are even too extreme like a pre-eployment licensing regime for frontier models and things like that um like an FDA style licensing regime is proposed right so uh I think that the once you you know I've often said that be uh like the real libertarians are fundamentally capability skeptics, right? Like I think that Adam Theier's deal is that he is a capability skeptic on this stuff, right? Like he just fundamentally does not believe this technology is going to be a nuke on your laptop, right? If he did, he would have different opinions. um in my view. >> Yeah. Maybe um going back to Ellen's initial positioning of us in the center between um the free market approach and the the so-called bureaucrats um radical optionality is not fixed to be always in the center between these two options. uh it can evolve over time and in fact radical optionality in part aims to put society in a position where they can move more towards uh one of these directions. If it becomes clearer over time, for instance, because assuming we build the sufficient capacity to understand what risk these systems are posing, sufficient capacity to to mitigate these risks, um then this should also move us closer to the free market approach um uh o o over time. So it's not uh rejecting that approach outright uh for eternity. It is more focused on we need we really need to understand these systems and what risk and govern and and and benefits they're posing um rather than ruling out a sort of um um um more towards the free market approaches or more towards a heavy regulatory approach. Um I think what we do want to rule out is a a sort of approach where these risks as is happening now with mythos are becoming clear somewhat last minute uh to relevant people in charge and then uh then like pulling an allnighter and trying to regulate this technology on on on short notice that seems to be destined to fail. Um I think the EU has gone like somewhat this pathway. Um I think they've gotten reasonably well out of it. Uh but if I remember back to November December 2023 where suddenly the um um during the the the trilog um it became more and more clear oh there are these foundation models. They're actually become quite becoming quite powerful. you can use them for various things and maybe they will become more powerful over time. Um um within the AI act negotiations, chapter five was introduced with is like this chapter on general purpose AI models with systemic risk that is sort of the EU's speech for frontier AI systems. Um and because they didn't have sufficient time, um what they basically did was like outlined the most common sense, uncontroversial positions, um but basically deferred making any sort of decisions to to a group um that was tasked with designing the code of practice. Um that was then just specifying what it means to to mitigate to identify and mitigate um some of these risks from these models. um but they were not in a position to decide for themselves um what what what this regulation should look like and I think would would be better if the United States didn't follow in the footsteps of the European Union and try to build the capacity before taking um significant regulatory steps. Uh maybe to some degree it's already a bit too late for that. Um but better start late than than never. >> Yeah. Well, I do want to spend some more time on that alternative position, the the the regulatory position, um because I think I agree with Charlie that there's been a a vibe shift, for lack of a better term, in the last few weeks, and I think it's a quite durable one in which the pure free market position is just off the table. No one takes it seriously anymore. And that the real debate is going to be over sort of what kind and how much regulation. Um AI is increasingly quite unpopular among many publiclix. You know, we're recording this actually the day after the release of the uh new paper encyclical about AI, which [clears throat] is a you know, whatever whatever you think about the diagnosis uh of AI's many potential flaws would require a quite muscular regulatory response uh to uh to uh address. Um why isn't the answer to go down that path more more fully, right? you know, why isn't the answer to adopt something like the European or it's not European the the precautionary principle that is often common in European policy discussions whereby you try to really minimize downside risks and you know given that AI could have national security implications given that AI could turn us all into paper clips right there are clearly clearly downside risks why why why isn't that the this the the safer and cleaner uh approach Kristoff >> so maybe to to start a response it's It's helpful to clarify the application of the precautionary principle under EU law works very different to the application and the discussion of the precautionary principle under uh in in US policy. um the application of the precautionary principle in the EU actually does include based on the European Commission's guideline a costbenefit analysis um um which um based on how the precautionary principle is viewed and understood in the US um would not happen um or at least to to to much um um lower extent. Um it does seem clear that you need uh some regulation um for systems like Mythos. Uh but it's also clear that what this overall governance regime looks like and will have to look like over time or should look like over time. Um we don't yet know this. Um and yes, we we we make certain policy um proposals that we think would be good to implement and are are lower risk and more common sense than various other proposals. These proposals in part are designed to for governments to develop the expertise about how to regulate AI over time. um if we too quickly regulate too heavily uh not just with regards to national security, we can leave this aside for a second. Um, of course you need to make sure that national security is protected um, appropriately. But if if you go very significantly beyond that without understanding how the technology works, without understanding um what kind of policies have what kind of effects on the industry of how this technology is developed in the United States versus in China um without understanding um without having the expertise within governments to enforce the various provisions that you also have uncertainty about. Um that seems tricky to me. Um um and it it's even more important because as you suggest we we are already getting late um to try to to to build these institutions within governments that can actually uh a regulate but then b also enforce in a sensible way. Um and in part of that is what's needed is like sufficiently flexible definitions, sufficiently wellunded institutions and so on and so forth. >> All right. So let's now talk about what radical optionality means concretely because it is a set of sort of policy suggestions um that governments should should take. Um and so let me start kind of we're not going to go through all of them. Um and I encourage folks to read the paper. It's very readable um uh and very interesting. I want to kind of hit the the highlights. So I'm going to ask each of you um and Charlie you can go first. What do you think is the most important of your proposals? Or to put it this way, which of your proposals is most radical and and how? Because I do want to focus on this idea that you're not all just advocating for optionality, but something called radical optionality. And I want to sort of tie that to the specific proposals that you make. >> Yeah. So the proposals individually, right, are designed to be things that could actually be implemented in the near-term future, right? Realistically, I'm not saying that they all will be, but maybe they can be. Um, and so because of that, uh, on their own, they might seem sort of like mild, right? None of them is like a licensing regime, right? None of them is like a ban on developing, uh, advanced artificial intelligence models or anything like that. Um I think that um the the radical thing about them is the scale uh at which we're we're proposing this should be done right so uh we're proposing that almost governments should be willing to spend almost an unlimited amount right almost uh not not quite unlimited but an an extraordinary amount of uh of money of political capital of resources in general uh to do anything that seems like it's likely to have a net positive effect on how this transition to a uh transformed AI future goes. Um and so in that light, I think that um something like uh the emphasis on hiring and talent that we have, right, uh is pretty radical because we're not merely suggesting, okay, there should be a an agency within government that uh is charged with uh overseeing how AI is developed, right, with with regulating if regulation becomes necessary and with, you know, uh setting standards and so forth. In the meantime, uh what we're proposing in fact is that uh you know uh at a bare minimum this kind of agency, this capacity within government should be funded to a similar level um as the level that we currently have for regulating, you know, new drugs, right? Or that we currently have for uh regulating airplanes, right? The the FAA in the US or the FDA. Um >> I mean, do you think that I'm just curious though, just to jump in, do you think that would even be enough, right? I mean, is is that radical enough? I mean, if you think about the kind of pay packages that your average machine learning, even your median machine learning engineer, forget your sort of 10x machine learning engineer is getting in Silicon Valley. Um, I mean, is is there, you know, how much of GDP will the government have to spend to staff up? Um, >> yeah. And I I think that's a Yeah, I I would let's let's put it this way. Uh, I think it would both be inadequate to the scale of the challenge and it would it's wildly unrealistic because it's far more than we're actually going to spend on any of this stuff in the short term, right? I think that it's maybe a little bit um it's I think there's some natural advantage in that like the the billion dollar pay packages that you see for example for for some top engineers that you saw six months ago for that kind of thing. Um I think that government has a natural hiring advantage and that it's like you know public service. there are people who are willing to take a pay cut to do public service. And also you don't necessarily need the very very very top machine learning engineers to be working in government. I think it's natural that the like uh people most concerned with making uh ungodly sums of money would be creating the technology and that people uh most concerned with seeing that it goes well um would would would be on the governance side. Um I think that like you know my understanding is that pay packages for uh sort of uh lab policy teams are not always quite as uh insane as they are for for uh you know uh engineers working on capabilities. So, uh, yeah, I I think that like funding, uh, an FDA for AI or whatever, that's probably the wrong comparison, but an agency charged with regulating AI, funding it like we fund, you know, the FAA or the FDA or other major uh, organizations, agencies in the government that have billions of dollars a year in funding, um, would be uh, a great start, let's put it that way. Um and since the sort of regulatory apparatus is less developed uh and we are you know trying to do less at the moment um even though the importance of the technology is greater might make sense uh or you know not necessarily make sense but it might be uh inevitable that uh that the total spend would be less at least at least at the start. >> Um if I had to pick one of these uh policy proposals that we are suggesting it would be the focus on hiring and talent more broadly. Um um we include talent as a broader category than just hiring because I think hiring is will not be enough. You need to attract people in various different ways and you need to be unusually creative about how to get expertise into into governments. Um um um the UN and the EU have established a scientific panels um which they've used to attract some like worldclass experts um to their institutions. It would be good if the US would do something similar. I could imagine they um will attract even more talented uh people. Um I could imagine that instead of the typical hiring or in addition to the typical hiring uh you will need to give out very significant contracts and by that you set the incentives to establish for instance a third party evaluations ecosystem and make it very attractive to start organizations whose main task is to um figure out how these systems are working, what risk they are producing and make sure that the information flows to governments in a way that governments can actually proc access the information. A because they have access to the information and B because they have the expertise to evaluate uh these. I do think that governments will also need to be unusually creative when it comes to hiring itself. Um and they should not shy away from um um changing the rules about how much they are um able to to to pay the people they they hire. Um, we don't need to go to the like $200 million a year salary packages or so, but they should be significantly higher. I think the UK AI security institute, all things considered, has done a pretty good job at at at at building institutional capacity to evaluate these systems uh uh carefully. Um uh but uh I don't see any reason why uh the EU, leave alone the United States, um should only aspire to be as good as the UK Air Security Institute. And currently it's very far from being that good. It should be 10 times as good. It should be 10 times as funded. It should attract the people that are currently going, the Americans that are currently going to foreign government to evaluate what their systems are capable of. um that to me seems like things have gone in in in in the wrong direction and it would be good to build this institutional capacity within the US. >> Okay. So let's say we have we have solved or or we have at least made some progress on this. We have managed to hire through a combination of increased salaries and good working conditions and spree core and you know mission um a bunch of really talented engineers and analysts and so forth. So what are they going to do right like what what are what is this FDA right? And again, I'm not I'm not going to hold you to the sort of that analogy, right? Um but what is this organization going to do? Because if you look at the regulatory organizations right now that deal with difficult problems, you know, whether it's the FDA for drugs or the FAA for airplanes or the SEC for securities, right? Yeah, they do some research, but they're not primarily research organizations. They are primarily regulatory organizations. So what what exactly what exactly is this uh group of really smart people going to do that is going to develop right that that is worth the the radical adjective here of optionality which is to say that is worth spending a lot of you know blood and treasure uh uh uh uh on >> hopefully not too much blood hopefully we'll we'll keep it limited to treasure although you know you never know um okay uh no I think that um the the sort of easy answer right there are various things that everybody always says. Everybody always says, "Oh, the uh science of EVELs is in its infancy, right? Uh we don't have good enough benchmarks. Everything's saturated. It's so hard to, you know, you hear this 17 times a day if you work in a policy, right? Okay. So, one easy solution is, okay, we're going to do government-run assessments and evaluations of frontier models and we're going to be we're going to make sure they're very competent. We're going to make new benchmarks. We're going to come up with new tools in order to conduct these measurements. There's gonna be a whole division of this agency that's devoted to that, right? And critically, this is an extremely light touch intervention, right? It doesn't inhibit innovation in any way. All it does is uh spend government resources on something the labs are already working on, right? And that the private market is doing an okay job of, but maybe is not allocating as many resources uh to as is socially optimal. Um so testing evals essentially is one answer, right? And standard setting, right? Another answer is okay. Um uh coming up with uh rules for uh government procurement of AI, right? uh government agencies including the military, including the NSA, including the intelligence community, including uh you know, lots of critical parts of government that aren't aren't classified are using AI in some pretty cutting edge applications. Um and there are lots of questions around okay, uh concentration of power risks from that, right? This is something that you've written on a lot, Alan. Um so there is work to be done in coming up with uh ways uh to make sure that that goes safely, right? So something like um you know in the US we have a set of cyber security standards. So if the department of war is going to produce is going to procure some sort of uh technology system, right? Uh the vendor procuring it uh for them has to uh abide by certain cyber security uh uh it's called CMMC, right? This this cyber security certification that you have to get in order to provide stuff to the US military to make that you're not compromised by, you know, adversaries or whatever, right? Something similar to that for AI would be great, right? just making sure that the uh that the programs are safe, that they're uh not going to uh be uh insufficiently law following uh all this sort of thing I think would be a would be a great start. That's one one thing that this organization could could start out by doing. >> Maybe just adding two things briefly. Um, one additional thing that this organization or group of people should be focusing on starting like as soon as possible would be to build relationships with other such institutions um in in other liberal democracies. Um, in part because I imagine that as AI continues to develop, uh, the stakes will get higher and higher and some of the really thorny questions will require at some point international cooperation and in order for international cooperation to work in any meaningful way. You need to have established trust and you don't do this overnight. Um, it will also require some established trust on the working level and not just on on leadership level. um um assuming that that will be there. Uh that's a that that's a big if. Um and then maybe secondly, as a sort of more meta response to your question, um it's not obvious to me that you actually have to have all of this positioned in one regulatory agency. It could also well be the case that you need different groups within a government, one tasked with regulating and one tasked with understanding what the heck is going on. It seems to me that the UK Air Security Institute that doesn't have regulatory powers um is doing a good job on evaluating um what what what is currently going on um versus the and and has a sort of like unique benefits uh that for instance the AI office that has the regulatory powers or at least the enforcement powers I think will um um um they're able to enforce um from August this year onwards. Um and there are sort of unique benefits if you don't have regulatory powers. Uh one is that you can um interact with companies in a different way. Uh and and be able to build more trust. Um in the end we we're like we are all in this together. Um and you you will need to be able to get some information um and build some trustworthy relationships u with employees in in companies. and and that seems to me easier um if you don't have regulatory powers. Um that still needs to happen elsewhere, but it's not obvious that this should be within the same institution. Let's get in the weed just for a second. Um I want to talk about the preeemption argument that you all make or the anti-pree preeemption argument that you all make and obviously Charlie is something that you've thought a lot about and you and I have gone uh uh gone back and forth in this a little bit. uh you know you you put forward a a quite well-developed argument that um in the debates that pop up you know periodically in DC about should there be federal preeemption of state AI regulation um you come down pretty strongly against that. Now let's put aside the sort of merits of preeemption generally for a second. I'm curious how this fits within your broader approach because to me preeemption is a kind of optionality um uh or at least it can be argued to be a kind of optionality mechanism uh because um you know laws once written are often very sticky. Laws once written create incentives that through past dependency themselves create the future. And so it seems to me at least plausibly that if if you're going to really go in on the optionality side of it, you you want you want preeemption. Uh so that the the brilliant people that you know we've all managed to hire through the hiring and talent process um can think about these issues sort of unencumbered by like Minnesota's view on X Y or Z, right? Or you know California's decision to do this that or the other thing. >> Yeah. So um you you makes you make a good point and this is one of the nice things about optionality is that everyone can argue that their preferred position is the one that maximizes optionality right for any given issue right >> which is which is meta optionality it's very clever how you >> you always have the option of arguing that this is optionality >> um >> the my my view is this right my view is that like um you know and I I I have have gone back and forth with the the one of the normal co-hosts of this uh podcast Kevin Frasier at a number different, you know, we've done debates on this at at Harvard Law School and Stanford Law School and I think next year we're doing one at Yale Law School. So, we're going to go down the the US News and World Report list of of law schools and debate preemption at every one of them by the end of 2028 or something. Um but uh the the the point that I usually make is like look historically right uh it is the case that uh for general purpose really important general purpose technologies like say uh electricity or the internet or AI right as as I suppose will be the case in the future um there has been an allocation of regulatory responsibility between states and the federal government it hasn't been all one or all the other right it's not even uh it's hard to pick any technology even incredibly interstate technologies ies like airline travel or something like that. It's hard to pick any technology where it's all state or all federal, right? Generally speaking, there is some level of state regulation for local harms, local risks, local benefits, and some level of federal regulation to address sort of like interstate issues. Um, my point is not that there shouldn't be preeemption. I'm not anti-premption, right? I think it's necessary and inevitable that there will be some federal preeemption of state laws. uh I just think that that should uh come later uh and as part of a uh as part of an iterative process of figuring out how regulatory authority of AI should be allocated between states and federal government. So for example, um I think it's like very probable that uh I I think it's optionality enhancing because uh if you pass something like the uh the most recent moratorum that's been intro introduced that uh that um that said you know last summer okay for 10 years no state will regulate AI in any way right no generally no non-generally applicable state law regulating AI can be enforced by states that would I think be like historically unprecedented and kind of radical because you're shutting off this whole wide area of like potential state regulation, right? I think by contrast, taking a look, okay, we don't like this kind of state law, right? Let's say that it's um you know uh these state laws that some people don't like that are uh prohibiting chatbots from acting as therapists or something, right? You could narrowly preempt those laws, getting rid of them, right? because we sort of understand what they are uh and we don't like them perhaps some people don't like them uh as opposed to just saying okay if you say all state laws regular AI are now preempted for 10 years then that's 10 years of like um you've preempted state laws that you couldn't even have anticipated the need for right if if if local harms come up from uh super capable general purpose AI systems uh and it and you start thinking gee it would be really great for states to be able to do a sort of laboratory democracy thing on these um you forclosed that possibility sort of before you even understood what the what the uh need for it was. Uh that's that's my my pitch on optionality in preemption. >> Related to some of Charlie's points, um I think state laws themselves offer lots of information value and from an optionality perspective, information value is extremely important. Um we still don't know we've debated this for years. Um but we still don't know uh whether entity based regulation or model based regulation or use based regulation and in what sort of instance which which is better. Uh these are some like really basic questions. Uh how do we define what is a frontier company or what is a frontier model? Um and and by just having some state legislation out there um that aims to to regulate the space, you do get quite a bit of information on what regulation works and what doesn't. I do think that your point with regards to the stickiness of of some of this is is is important to take into consideration which is why I'm directionally more open here to to sunset clauses as like Kevin I believe suggested on on on multiple occasions than than other domains. Um in part because the value of this regulation itself is to get information on what regulation works. >> I think it's a good place to end it. It's a really terrific paper and I really encourage uh folks to read it. Uh Kristoff, Charlie, thanks for coming on the show to talk about it. >> Thanks for having us. >> Thanks so much.
